title: Using a shaving horse
subtitle: Foot-pedal clamping for drawknife and spokeshave work
excerpt: The shaving horse clamps the workpiece with foot pressure, leaving both hands free for a drawknife or spokeshave. This tutorial covers setting the bench height, positioning the workpiece in the dumbhead, the foot-pedal clamp action, and the drawknife stroke used from the horse.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. II (1883) for the shaving-horse design and working posture. Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898) for drawknife use at the horse.

content.text: The
shaving horse
is the defining tool of the English green-woodworking tradition. It allows a maker to shape a riven stave, a chair leg, or a spoon blank with a
drawknife

at speed, using both hands for the cut while foot pressure holds the work. The technique requires no vices, no clamps, and no second pair of hands. A skilled bodger at the horse can shape a chair leg from a riven
green-wood
billet in a few minutes.
When to use the shaving horse

The shaving horse handles long, straight, or gently tapered work that benefits from a drawknife rather than an axe or knife. Chair legs, stretchers, stool legs, rake handles, mallet handles, long spoon blanks, and the staves for a basket are all natural candidates. For very short pieces (under 15 cm), the dumbhead grip becomes awkward and a vise or handscrew may be more practical.
Setting up the horse

Sit astride the bench seat with both feet on the treadle. The bench height is correct when, sitting naturally on the seat, the treadle is comfortable to press with both feet flat on it. Too low and you will hunch; too high and you cannot press the treadle without raising off the seat. Most traditional shaving horses are adjustable in seat height; set it once for your leg length and leave it.
The technique
Step 1: Load and clamp the workpiece

Lift the dumbhead bar by releasing foot pressure from the treadle. Slide the workpiece into the slot between the dumbhead and the bed. Position the workpiece so the section to be cut in this stroke is just in front of the dumbhead, extending toward you. Press down firmly with both feet on the treadle. The dumbhead should grip the workpiece firmly. Test by pulling the workpiece toward you: it should not slide. If it does, reposition the treadle angle or raise the dumbhead height on the adjustment screw.
Step 2: Set the drawknife

Hold the drawknife with both hands on the handles. Bevel up (toward you) for a shaving cut. Bevel down for a roughing cut. Set the blade on the workpiece at the far end of the section to be cut. Tilt the bevel to the shaving angle you want, usually 20 to 30 degrees from the surface for a shaping cut.
Step 3: Draw the stroke

Pull the drawknife toward the body in a smooth, even stroke. Both forearms should move together; do not pull predominantly with one hand or the cut will skew. The shaving lifts and curls away from the bevel. Each stroke removes material from the far end of the clamped section to the near end. Reposition the workpiece forward by releasing foot pressure, sliding the piece toward you, and re-clamping. Repeat until the desired profile is reached.
Grain direction at the horse

As with all hand-tool cuts, the drawknife must travel with the grain, not against it. On a straight riven stave the grain runs parallel to the face and the direction of travel is straightforward. On a tapered or curved piece, the grain will often run uphill on one side and downhill on the other. A cut going uphill against the grain tears rather than shaves; the surface looks furry rather than smooth and the blade tends to dive into the wood. Reverse the workpiece end-for-end in the dumbhead and take the stroke from the other direction.

attrs.intro: Common problems at the shaving horse.

items.fix: Check the treadle adjustment first. If the horse is correctly adjusted, put a piece of sandpaper between the dumbhead and the workpiece for grip.
items.cause: Foot pressure is too light, or the treadle angle is wrong for the maker's leg length. Or the workpiece is too smooth and the dumbhead face cannot grip it.
items.symptom: The workpiece slips in the dumbhead during the drawknife stroke

items.fix: Release, reposition the workpiece so the grain runs in the correct direction, and try a light stroke from the other end. Check that the blade bevel is facing upward for a shaving cut.
items.cause: The cut is going against the grain. The blade is following a downhill grain line into the wood rather than along the surface.
items.symptom: The drawknife dives into the wood rather than shaving across it

items.fix: Strop the drawknife edge. If the wood is dry, switch to a spokeshave or card scraper for the finishing work.
items.cause: The drawknife edge is dull, or the wood is too dry for drawknife work.
items.symptom: The shaving is rough and fibrous rather than smooth

attrs.heading: Common problems
